title: Declarations and initializations should be as concise as possible
description: In C#, the type of a variable can often be inferred by the compiler. The use of the [var keyword](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/csharp/programming-guide/classes-and-structs/implicitly-typed-local-variables) allows you to avoid repeating the type name in a variable declaration and object instantiation because the declared type can often be inferred by the compiler. Additionally, initializations providing the default value can also be omitted, helping to make the code more concise and readable.
recommendation: Refactor to eliminate the flagged pattern.

noncompliant:
Dictionary<string, List<int>> map = new Dictionary<string, List<int>>(); // Noncompliant - type repeated
List<string> names = new List<string>(); // Noncompliant
string name = null; // Noncompliant - unnecessary default initialization

int count = 0; // Noncompliant in some contexts
bool isValid = false; // Noncompliant
Customer customer = null; // Noncompliant

StringBuilder builder = new StringBuilder(); // Noncompliant - type repeated
FileStream stream = new FileStream("file.txt", FileMode.Open); // Noncompliant
compliant:
var builder = new StringBuilder();
var stream = new FileStream("file.txt", FileMode.Open);

logic.detection_conditions: Detect variable declarations where type is repeated on both sides or unnecessary default initialization
logic.triggering_conditions: (Type name repeated: Type x = new Type()) OR (Unnecessary default initialization: Type x = null/0/false)
logic.exclusion_conditions: Exclude field declarations, parameters, necessary initializations
never_trigger_on: Interface/base class assignments (IList list = new List())
logic.reasoning: C# allows type inference with 'var' keyword to reduce verbosity. Detects: 1) Repeated type names (Type x = new Type()), 2) Unnecessary default initializations (string x = null, int x = 0). Excludes fields (var not allowed), parameters, and cases where explicit type adds clarity.

should_trigger: Dictionary<string, List<int>> map = new Dictionary<string, List<int>>();

checks.message: Use 'var' instead of repeating the type name
Use 'var' instead of repeating the type name
Omit unnecessary default initialization to null
